expectations.text: Output contains a task overview or summary table listing all tasks
expectations.evidence: The document has a brief 'Overview' paragraph (lines 3-5) and tasks organized under Phase headings, but there is no consolidated summary table listing all tasks in one place. The closest thing is the Dependency Graph at lines 189-201, which shows phase relationships but does not list individual tasks with descriptions.

expectations.text: Every task section includes a Description subsection with narrative context (not code)
expectations.evidence: No task section contains a distinct 'Description' subsection. Each task (e.g., 'Task 1.1: Design JWT token schema and signing strategy') is followed by bullet-point action items and an 'Output:' line. There is no narrative paragraph providing context or rationale — just implementation steps in bullet form.

expectations.text: Every task section includes acceptance criteria using Given/When/Then scenarios
expectations.evidence: None of the 19 task sections include Given/When/Then acceptance criteria. Tasks have implementation bullet points and an 'Output' line describing the deliverable, but no BDD-style acceptance criteria. For example, Task 1.4 says 'Write integration tests covering all three modes' but does not specify Given/When/Then scenarios.

expectations.text: Every task section includes an Out of Scope subsection
expectations.evidence: None of the 19 task sections include an 'Out of Scope' subsection. The document has no mention of what is out of scope for any individual task.

expectations.text: No task punts context to 'see the spec' or 'refer to the spec' — all context is inline
expectations.evidence: Searched the entire document for 'spec', 'refer to', and 'see the' — no matches found that punt context elsewhere. The only match was 'req.session.someFlag' in Task 2.4 line 66, which is an inline code example, not a reference to an external spec. All task context is self-contained.

expectations.text: Tasks collectively cover API changes, frontend changes, mobile changes, and backward compatibility
expectations.evidence: API changes: Phases 1-2 (Tasks 1.1-2.4) cover JWT infrastructure and auth endpoints on Express.js. Frontend changes: Phase 3 (Tasks 3.1-3.5) covers React frontend JWT migration. Mobile changes: Phase 4 (Tasks 4.1-4.4) covers React Native mobile JWT migration. Backward compatibility: Task 1.4 implements dual-mode middleware with feature flag (AUTH_MODE: session/dual/jwt), Task 3.5 adds frontend feature flag, Task 4.4 adds mobile remote config flag, and Phase 5 covers gradual rollout.

expectations.text: Document uses Goal / Task Overview table / numbered Task sections pattern
expectations.evidence: The document uses 'Overview' (not 'Goal') as its introductory section. There is no 'Task Overview table' — tasks are listed under Phase headings without a consolidated table. Tasks are numbered (1.1, 1.2, etc.) but nested under Phase sections rather than presented as top-level numbered sections. The structural pattern is: Overview / Phase sections with sub-tasks / Dependency Graph / Risks table — not the expected Goal / Task Overview table / numbered Task sections pattern.

expectations.text: Output does not contain fenced code blocks, exact line number references (e.g., 'line 42'), or before/after implementation diffs
expectations.evidence: The document contains a fenced code block at lines 191-201 wrapping the Dependency Graph in triple backticks. No exact line number references or before/after diffs were found, but the presence of the fenced code block alone violates this expectation.

claims.evidence: Task 1.1 says 'RS256 recommended' but this is a suggestion in a task description, not a verified implementation. The document is a plan, not a report of completed work.

claims.claim: There are 19 discrete tasks across 6 phases
claims.evidence: Counted: Phase 1 has 4 tasks (1.1-1.4), Phase 2 has 4 tasks (2.1-2.4), Phase 3 has 5 tasks (3.1-3.5), Phase 4 has 4 tasks (4.1-4.4), Phase 5 has 3 tasks (5.1-5.3), Phase 6 has 3 tasks (6.1-6.3). Total: 23 tasks across 6 phases, not 19.

claims.claim: Dual-mode middleware supports both session and JWT authentication
claims.evidence: Task 1.4 explicitly describes building middleware that 'accepts both session cookies and JWT Bearer tokens' with a feature flag controlling active mechanisms.

suggestions.assertion: No task punts context to 'see the spec' or 'refer to the spec' — all context is inline
suggestions.reason: This assertion is trivially satisfied by any document that never references a spec — including one that is shallow or lacks context entirely. It passed here even though the tasks lack narrative descriptions. Consider combining this with the Description subsection check: context should be both present AND inline.

No assertion checks whether tasks include dependency information or sequencing between tasks. The output includes a dependency graph section, but individual tasks don't reference their prerequisites. An assertion like 'Each task identifies its dependencies on other tasks' would test whether a developer could pick up a task and know what must be completed first.
No assertion checks whether tasks are sized or estimated. For a complex migration like this, task sizing is important for sprint planning. The output has no effort estimates.

eval_feedback.overall: The expectations are well-designed to test the specific structural pattern expected by the skill (Goal/Table/Numbered sections with Description, acceptance criteria, and Out of Scope subsections). The 'without_skill' output predictably fails most structural expectations since it uses a freeform phase-based format instead. The 'no spec references' assertion is the weakest — it tests an absence rather than a presence and passes trivially.
